retrieved_docs.title: Erythromycin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a prolonged QT interval on an electrocardiogram (ECG) should not use erythromycin. A normal QTc interval would be less than 440 ms in males and less than 460 ms in females. Anyone using a medication that prolongs the QT interval should be very cautious and monitor if adding erythromycin. Similarly, patients with long QT syndrome (LQTS) should not use erythromycin. Patients who have had a past medical history of episodes of torsades de pointes should avoid QT-prolonging drugs such as erythromycin. Erythromycin is contraindicated in patients taking terfenadine, astemizole, or cisapride. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: QT Prolonging Drugs -- Indications -- QT-Prolonging Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Many commonly used medications, such as diphenhydramine and azithromycin, exhibit QT-prolonging effects. However, the degree of QT prolongation is not severe enough to warrant immediate caution in healthy patients. These medications bind to the human ether-related gene (hERG) channels and reduce electrical conduction through the potassium ion channels. This results in delayed repolarization of the heart. [11] The actual risk of torsades de pointes remains unknown with these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs/medications that cause QT prolongation: Clarithromycin Erythromycin Metoclopramide Haloperidol Methadone Droperidol Fluoroquinolone antibiotics (i.e., ciprofloxacin) Antiemetics (i.e., ondansetron) Antiarrhythmics Other causes: Hypovolemia Hypoxia Acidosis Hypothermia Tension pneumothorax Cardiac tamponade Pulmonary embolism

retrieved_docs.title: Erythromycin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin causes common adverse drug reactions such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and serious adverse drug reactions such as QT prolongation and pseudomembranous colitis. The clinician who is prescribing erythromycin should monitor the patient for QT prolongation. Torsades de pointes require the immediate cessation of therapy, referral to a cardiologist, and prompt treatment. Pseudomembranous colitis requires referral to a gastroenterologist. Pharmacists should check for potential drug-drug interactions as erythromycin is a known enzyme inhibitor. Pharmacists should also ensure proper medication reconciliation. The nurses can play a vital role in medication administration and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: QT Prolonging Drugs -- Indications -- QT-Prolonging Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics: Haloperidol, ziprasidone, quetiapine, thioridazine, olanzapine, risperidone, droperidol Antiarrhythmics: Amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide, procainamide, quinidine, flecainide Antibiotics: Macrolides, fluoroquinolones Antidepressants: Amitriptyline, imipramine, citalopram Others: Methadone, sumatriptan, ondansetron, cisapride

retrieved_docs.title: Fexofenadine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the findings of these studies, some case reports have suggested a potential association between fexofenadine and QT-interval elongation and ventricular arrhythmia. Concurrent use of fexofenadine with hepatic CYP3A4 blockers, such as erythromycin and ketoconazole, may elevate blood concentration levels. In contrast, combined use with drugs such as rifampin and troglitazone can decrease concentration levels. These effects are likely attributed to interactions between these compounds and the P-gp transporter, which facilitates the removal of fexofenadine from the bloodstream. [45]

retrieved_docs.content: The use of haloperidol with protease inhibitors (PI) or azithromycin with ZDV can lead to an increase in QTc and result in cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.content: QT prolongation [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dofetilide -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: QT-prolonging drugs: Class III antiarrhythmics are QTc-prolonging agents, and their use may significantly increase QTc interval prolongation. Combining these drugs with other QT-prolonging agents, such as erythromycin, can further elevate the risk of severe toxicities, including torsades de pointes, though evidence on such combinations remains limited. Drugs like dofetilide, methadone, chlorpromazine, sotalol, amiodarone, procainamide, ziprasidone, quinidine, disopyramide, cisapride, quinine, dronedarone, and bedaquiline, among others, may amplify this risk. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Rufinamide -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Minor contraindications include concomitant use of rufinamide with other potential drugs that shorten QT interval and the presence or history of the short QT interval; therefore, the recommendation is to routinely check the QT interval in these patient populations. Other contraindications include hypersensitivity to rufinamide and/or triazole derivatives.

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular agents (ivabradine): bradycardia, conduction disturbances

retrieved_docs.title: Quinidine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: QT-prolonging drugs, such as clarithromycin and amiodarone, increase TdP risk when administered with quinidine. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythromycin -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: It may also increase the risk of pyloric stenosis in the newborn. All antibiotics carry a significant risk of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. Erythromycin is a motilin agonist, which increases the likelihood of gastrointestinal side effects compared to other antibiotics. [18] All macrolide antibiotics cause QT prolongation. Azithromycin causes clinically insignificant QT prolongation. Clarithromycin causes greater QT prolongation. Erythromycin is known to cause significant prolongation of the QT interval and carries a risk of torsades de pointes. [19] This arrhythmia may cease on its own or may degenerate into ventricular fibrillation, which can be fatal. [20] There also exists a risk of rash, allergic reaction, and reversible deafness. Rare side effects include Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Ivabradine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: CYP3A4 inhibitors: CYP3A4 metabolizes ivabradine. Administering CYP3A4 inhibitors increases ivabradine plasma concentrations, while CYP3A4 inducers reduce them. Increased ivabradine levels may potentiate bradycardia and conduction abnormalities. The co-administration of potent CYP3A4 inhibitors with ivabradine is contraindicated. Strong inhibitors include azole antifungals such as itraconazole, macrolide antibiotics such as telithromycin and clarithromycin, HIV protease inhibitors, and nefazodone. [36] Verapamil and diltiazem are also CYP3A4 inhibitors; they increase ivabradine levels and, hence, the risk of bradycardia. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Dofetilide -- Administration -- Steps for Individualized Dose Initiation:
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequent Doses : Monitor QTc or QT after each dose without further reductions based on QTc or QT after the second dose.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 307
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydroergotamin should not be co‑administered with erythromycin because the two drugs are contraindicated with each other. Erythromycin is also contraindicated with simvastatin, indicating that simultaneous use of these agents can lead to harmful interactions. In addition, nelfinavir mesylate is contraindicated with simvastatin, so this combination should be avoided as well. Awareness of these drug‑drug contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse effects in patients receiving any of these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1910
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin and nelfinavir mesylate are both contraindicated with simvastatin, indicating that these agents should not be co‑administered because of the risk of significant drug‑drug interactions that can raise simvastatin concentrations and increase the likelihood of muscle toxicity such as myopathy or rhabdomyolysis. Clinicians should avoid prescribing erythromycin or nelfinavir mesylate together with simvastatin and consider alternative therapies or dose adjustments to ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin is classified within the broad parent category of anti‑infective agents for bacteria, viruses and fungi, indicating its role as a broad‑spectrum antimicrobial drug. This parent class situates erythromycin among agents used to treat a wide range of infectious organisms. In addition, dihydroergotamin is identified as a contraindicated class for erythromycin, meaning that co‑administration of erythromycin with dihydroergotamin is contraindicated due to the risk of adverse drug interactions. Clinicians should therefore avoid prescribing erythromycin to patients who are receiving dihydroergotamin to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Macrolides are contraindicated for use together with methylergonovine, indicating that co‑administration of this antibiotic class and the uterotonic agent methylergonovine should be avoided due to potential adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 685
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydroergotamin, erythromycin, simvastatin, cyklosporin, and nelfinavir mesylate are linked by several important drug‑drug contraindications. Dihydroergotamin should not be co‑administered with erythromycin because the two agents are contraindicated together. Erythromycin is also contraindicated with simvastatin, indicating that simultaneous use can increase the risk of adverse effects such as elevated statin concentrations. Simvastatin has additional contraindications with cyklosporin and with nelfinavir mesylate, reflecting a high potential for harmful interactions when these medications are combined. Recognizing and avoiding these contraindicated combinations is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone, an antiarrhythmic medication, is contraindicated for use with saquinavir, an HIV protease inhibitor, because co‑administration can lead to harmful drug interactions; therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing these two agents together to prevent adverse effects and ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class relationships and CYP3A4‑mediated contraindications are essential for safe prescribing and for preventing serious adverse events such as hypotension, excessive drug exposure, and statin‑related muscle toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin is classified as a member of the ophthalmologic antibiotics group, indicating its use in eye infections. Both erythromycin and nelfinavir mesylate are identified as contraindicated agents when co‑administered with simvastatin, reflecting a significant drug‑drug interaction risk. The concurrent use of either erythromycin or nelfinavir mesylate with simvastatin can elevate simvastatin concentrations, increasing the likelihood of adverse effects such as myopathy or rhabdomyolysis. Therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing these combinations or implement careful monitoring and dose adjustments to ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydroergotamine is contraindicated for use together with erythromycin, indicating that co‑administration of this migraine medication and the macrolide antibiotic should be avoided due to the risk of adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 227

Together, these interrelationships clarify how drug classes, individual agents, chemical families, therapeutic uses, mechanisms of action, contraindications and adverse‑effect profiles are organized, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating co‑occurring risks and monitoring relevant clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is contraindicated in patients receiving nelfinavir mesylate because of a significant drug interaction. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, which can alter the metabolism of other medications that are substrates of these enzymes. The combination of its contraindication with nelfinavir mesylate and its inhibitory effects on CYP2C9 and CYP3A highlights the importance of thorough medication review and monitoring when amiodarone is prescribed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 558
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydroergotamin is contraindicated for use together with erythromycin and also with posaconazole, indicating that co‑administration of these agents should be avoided. Erythromycin is likewise contraindicated when combined with simvastatin, and this interaction extends to other drugs that share the same risk profile: cyclosporine (cyklosporin) and nelfinavir mesylate are both listed as having a contraindicated class relationship with simvastatin, meaning that simvastatin should not be prescribed concurrently with either cyclosporine or nelfinavir mesylate. In addition to its drug‑interaction considerations, posaconazole is identified as belonging to the class of trypanocidal agents (trypanocidální látky). Recognizing these contraindications and the pharmacologic classification of posaconazole is essential for safe medication management and for preventing adverse drug events in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: Cyklosporin and nelfinavir mesylate are both identified as contraindicated when used together with simvastatin, indicating that co‑administration of either cyklosporin or nelfinavir mesylate with simvastatin should be avoided due to the risk of adverse drug interactions.
